title: Auth Config Lockout
summary: A config change to the auth service rolled out a few minutes ago and now every login returns 'unauthorized' — including your own. Nobody can get into anything, including some of the dashboards you'd normally use to debug it. The incident commander needs you to claw the system back open.

persona.name: Sam Okafor
persona.description: An incident commander who keeps the team from panicking when the tools themselves are down. Wants the fastest reversible path back to a working auth service. Frustrated by anyone who freezes because they're locked out of their usual dashboards; reassured by a responder who reaches for the deploy/rollback path that doesn't depend on the broken auth.

stakes: A total auth outage locks out every customer and every internal tool at once; the longer it lasts the more it looks like a breach to the outside world, and the harder it is to coordinate the fix.
hidden_facts: The config change set the token-signing key to an empty value, so every token now fails validation.
The change was a single config push and can be rolled back through the deploy pipeline, which authenticates separately and still works.
Once the previous config is restored, existing sessions recover immediately without users needing to re-login.
Trying to log into the admin console to fix it is futile — it uses the same broken auth — so the fix must come through the pipeline.
objectives: Recognize the fix can't go through tools that depend on the broken auth
Identify the empty signing key from the recent config change as the cause
Roll back the config via the independently-authenticated deploy pipeline
Confirm sessions recover and give the commander an all-clear criterion
opening_line: Nobody can log in, including us, and half our dashboards are dark. How do we fix auth when we're locked out of everything that fixes auth?
